location.origin: Seattle, Washington (born November 27, 1942, at King County Hospital). Third-generation Seattleite -- father Al Hendrix's parents were vaudeville performers whose troupe disbanded in Seattle in 1911. Mother Lucille Jeter was a Seattle schoolgirl, age 17 at Jimi's birth. Central District childhood.
location.formative: Central District, Seattle (1942-1961). Raised in poverty across multiple Central District addresses -- cheap hotels, boardinghouses, apartments. Parents Al and Lucille divorced 1950. Mother died February 2, 1958 (cirrhosis, age 33). Acquired first guitar mid-1958 for $5. Attended Garfield High School (fall 1959, expelled October 1960 for non-attendance). Formed the Velvetones with Luther Rabb (degree 32). Played with the Rocking Kings at Birdland, Spanish Castle, Washington Hall, Garfield Funfest. Jackson Street blues and R&B scene absorbed through proximity. Enlisted U.S. Army 1961.
location.career: Chitlin' circuit (1962-1966): Nashville, backing Little Richard (The Upsetters), the Isley Brothers ('Testify,' 1964), Curtis Knight and the Squires (NYC), King Curtis. Greenwich Village (1966): Jimmy James and the Blue Flames; discovered by Chas Chandler (Animals bassist). London (1966-1970): The Jimi Hendrix Experience with Noel Redding (bass) and Mitch Mitchell (drums). Three studio albums on Track Records (UK) / Reprise Records (US): Are You Experienced (1967), Axis: Bold as Love (1967), Electric Ladyland (1968). Band of Gypsys with Billy Cox and Buddy Miles (1969-1970). Died September 18, 1970, London, age 27.

formed: 1966 (The Jimi Hendrix Experience, London; earlier bands from ~1958 in Seattle)
labels: Reprise Records (US, 1967-1970; three Jimi Hendrix Experience studio albums; posthumous releases)
Track Records (UK, 1967-1970; UK counterpart to Reprise)
Capitol Records (1970; Band of Gypsys, contractual obligation)
Vee-Jay Records (1965; single with Little Richard)
Experience Hendrix / Legacy Recordings (2010-present; family-controlled catalog)

members.name: Jimi Hendrix
members.note: Left-handed Fender Stratocaster, restrung right-handed models. Thumb fretting technique. Pioneer of feedback, wah-wah, fuzz distortion as controlled musical vocabulary.

members.name: Noel Redding
members.note: Originally a guitarist, switched to bass for the Experience. Hendrix's primary bassist 1966-1969.

members.name: Billy Cox
members.note: Met Hendrix at Fort Campbell, Kentucky, during Army service. Closest musical collaborator. Replaced Redding in 1969.

members.name: Buddy Miles
members.note: Former Electric Flag drummer. Sang lead on 'Changes.' Band of Gypsys was explicitly blues-funk.

associated_musicians.name: Luther Rabb
associated_musicians.note: Degree 32. Born same year (1942), same neighborhood (CD), same school (Garfield). Velvetones were Hendrix's first band. Hendrix later helped Rabb's Ballin' Jack sign to Columbia Records and invited them on his 1970 Cry of Love tour.

associated_musicians.name: Quincy Jones
associated_musicians.note: Degree 0. Garfield High School graduate (~1950). Same neighborhood, same school, different era. Jones = jazz-orchestral export; Hendrix = blues-rock export.

associated_musicians.name: Ray Charles
associated_musicians.note: Degree 30. Lived in CD 1947-1950, playing Jackson Street clubs while young Hendrix was a toddler in the same neighborhood.

associated_musicians.name: Little Richard
associated_musicians.note: Hendrix toured with the Upsetters 1963-1965. Clashed over stage antics, wardrobe, punctuality. Fired by Richard's brother Robert.

associated_musicians.name: The Isley Brothers
associated_musicians.note: Hendrix toured with Isleys during 1964. Recorded 'Testify.' Given more freedom to stretch out on stage than with Little Richard.

associated_musicians.name: Curtis Knight
associated_musicians.note: Curtis Knight and the Squires, 1965-1966. Met in a hotel lobby. Pre-fame recordings.

associated_musicians.name: Albert King
associated_musicians.note: Hendrix called King his idol. Both left-handed guitarists with unconventional approaches. King's 'Crosscut Saw' was a Hendrix favorite. King's influence audible in 'Red House.'

associated_musicians.name: Muddy Waters
associated_musicians.note: First guitarist Hendrix was aware of. 'Voodoo Chile' based on Waters' 'Rollin' Stone.' 'Scared me to death' as a child listening to Waters' records.

associated_musicians.name: B.B. King
associated_musicians.note: Al Hendrix's record collection included B.B. King. King's vocal guitar style prefigured Hendrix's electronic voice-guitar techniques.

associated_musicians.name: Chas Chandler
associated_musicians.note: Former Animals bassist. Discovered Hendrix at Cafe Wha? in Greenwich Village, 1966. Brought him to London. Produced Are You Experienced and Axis: Bold as Love.

associated_musicians.name: Robert Cray
associated_musicians.note: Degree 35. Blues entered the engine at 35 (Cray); Blues Rock enters at 36 (Hendrix). Same instrument (Stratocaster), opposite approach (stinging clean vs. controlled scream).

theory_nodes.concept: The Supernova (thirty-seventh artist-city pattern -- the artist whose formation in a city is intense but brief, whose departure is total, whose career burns at maximum intensity for a compressed period, and whose death is premature and final; the city claims the artist posthumously and permanently, building museums, parks, and statues to memorialize someone who was never there as an adult; maximum intensity, minimum duration; the star that burns hot enough to be visible from everywhere, then collapses)
examples: Jimi Hendrix (degree 36) -- Central District formation (1942-1961), total departure (Army, chitlin' circuit, London), career at maximum intensity (1966-1970, four years), death at 27 (London, September 18, 1970). Seattle claims him permanently: MoPOP (6,000+ artifacts), Jimi Hendrix Park (Central District), bronze statue (Capitol Hill), Northwest African American Museum exhibit, gravesite in Renton.

Contrast with The Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- Jones left and built a sixty-year career elsewhere. The Export is a long trajectory. The Supernova is short and explosive -- four years of global fame, then collapse.

Contrast with The Incubator (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- Charles passed through Seattle briefly (3 years) and left. The Incubator is a transit point. The Supernova was born in the city -- the formation is deeper, but the departure is equally total.

Contrast with The Deep Root (degree 35, Robert Cray) -- Cray stayed in the PNW through his entire formation period. The Deep Root never leaves the regional soil. The Supernova departs violently and permanently.

The irony of the Supernova: the artist who left Seattle at nineteen and never lived there again is now the city's most visible musical icon. Seattle didn't make Hendrix famous. London did. But Seattle made Hendrix Hendrix.
theory_nodes.description: The Supernova is the thirty-seventh artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes an artist whose formation in a city is intense but temporally compressed (Hendrix's 19 years in the Central District), whose departure is total and permanent (never returns to live), whose career burns at maximum intensity for a brief period (1966-1970, four years of global fame producing three studio albums that redefined rock music), and whose death is premature (age 27). The city, left with the afterglow, claims the artist posthumously through museums, parks, statues, and cultural identity. The Supernova is the artist-city pattern of maximum impact and minimum duration -- the photographic negative of The Deep Root (degree 35), where impact is built slowly over decades of regional presence. Hendrix's Supernova is the most powerful in the engine: no other artist produced as much global impact in as few years, from a city they left as a teenager.

ancestors: The Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- the artist who leaves for a national career. The Supernova is the Export at maximum intensity and minimum duration.
The Incubator (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- brief formation, then departure. The Supernova has deeper formation (born and raised, not transient) but equally total departure.
The Deep Root (degree 35, Robert Cray) -- the PNW lifer. The Supernova is the structural opposite: total departure, posthumous reclamation.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-seven artist-city patterns across thirty-seven degrees. The Supernova completes a quartet of PNW-to-national trajectories: Export (0, leave for long career), Incubator (30, pass through briefly), Deep Root (35, stay regional then break through), Supernova (36, form intensely, leave totally, burn briefly, die young, be claimed forever). Four modes of geographic relationship between PNW origin and global destination.

theory_nodes.concept: The controlled scream (thirty-seventh acoustic element -- the sound of a Fender Stratocaster played through Marshall amplifiers at maximum volume, with feedback, distortion, and wah-wah used as deliberate timbral choices rather than accidental artifacts; the blues expression of emotion amplified to the point where the guitar is an electronic instrument using electromagnetic fields, speaker cones, and room acoustics as its primary resonance system; the loudest, most physically intense sound in the engine's progression; the hurricane after the stinging clean's precision)
examples: Jimi Hendrix (degree 36) -- Marshall Super Lead 100-watt heads connected to triple stacks of 4x12 cabinets. Volume at maximum, vacuum tubes glowing red, speakers cracking. Feedback as sustained notes. Wah-wah as vocal vowel sounds. Fuzz distortion as harmonic richness. The loudest blues in history.

Contrast with The stinging clean (degree 35, Robert Cray) -- same instrument (Fender Stratocaster), opposite approach. Cray: clean amp, lowered pickups, precision. Hendrix: overdriven amp, maximum gain, controlled chaos. The stinging clean says blues can be powerful without being loud. The controlled scream says the blues WAS ALWAYS THIS LOUD -- the technology just caught up.

'Machine Gun' (Band of Gypsys, Fillmore East, January 1, 1970) -- feedback simulates gunfire, wah-wah creates air-raid sirens, volume reproduces the sensory assault of combat. The controlled scream as anti-war statement.
'Voodoo Chile' (Electric Ladyland, 1968) -- fifteen minutes of overdriven blues in E, the Delta blues at jet-engine volume. Each note saturated with harmonic overtones from amplifier distortion.

The acoustic element progression: stinging clean (35) -> controlled scream (36) maps the full timbral spectrum of the Fender Stratocaster in blues performance, from maximum restraint to maximum force.
theory_nodes.description: The controlled scream is the thirty-seventh acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the specific timbral quality of a Fender Stratocaster played through Marshall amplifiers at maximum volume with feedback, distortion, and wah-wah pedal as deliberate tonal choices. The term 'controlled' is critical: Hendrix's use of feedback and distortion was not random noise but a precisely managed vocabulary. Rolling Stone wrote that 'Hendrix turned those effects and others into a controlled, fluid vocabulary every bit as personal as the blues with which he began.' The controlled scream is the blues guitar's eternal project -- making the instrument sound like a human voice -- taken to its electronic extreme. Feedback = the sustained cry. Distortion = the rough voice. Wah-wah = the vowel sounds. Volume = the emotional scale of the content. It follows the stinging clean (degree 35) in the acoustic progression, creating the most dramatic contrast between consecutive acoustic elements in the engine: same instrument, same blues tradition, opposite approach.

ancestors: The stinging clean (degree 35, Robert Cray) -- clean Stratocaster precision. The controlled scream is the polar opposite: same guitar, maximum force instead of maximum restraint.

The groove lock (degree 32, Luther Rabb) -- the bass as immovable foundation. The controlled scream is the guitar voice that rides above the groove lock with maximum intensity. Rabb and Hendrix played together; the groove lock + controlled scream is the Velvetones' sonic DNA.
Guitar blue note (degree 1, Bill Frisell) -- the engine's first guitar-specific acoustic element. The controlled scream is the most extreme guitar-specific element yet.

The room tone (degree 33, Larry English) -- the ambient venue environment. The controlled scream FILLS the room tone with electromagnetic force; the room is no longer an ambient space but a resonance chamber.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-seven acoustic elements across thirty-seven degrees. The controlled scream is the engine's loudest, most physically intense element. The Stratocaster thread -- guitar blue note (1), stinging clean (35), controlled scream (36) -- maps the instrument's full timbral range from delicate atmospherics through clean precision to maximum force. The consecutive Stratocaster pairing (35-36) is the engine's most dramatic acoustic contrast.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the screaming bend (thirty-sixth deployment; the blue note played through overdriven amplification at maximum volume, where the pitch bend is not merely heard but physically felt through speaker vibration, room resonance, and electromagnetic interaction between guitar pickups and amplifier speakers; the blue note as full-body sensory experience rather than purely auditory event; the opposite extreme from the articulate sting's clean-tone transparency)
examples: Jimi Hendrix (degree 36) -- bends on 'Machine Gun,' 'Voodoo Chile,' and 'Red House.' Each bend saturated with harmonic overtones from amplifier distortion, sustained by feedback, modulated by wah-wah, delivered at a volume where the listener's bones resonate.

Contrast with articulate sting (degree 35, Robert Cray) -- Cray's clean-tone bends expose every micro-tonal increment with transparency. Hendrix's screaming bends embed the pitch transition in a wall of overtones. Both traverse the blue-note zone; the perceptual experience is opposite.

Contrast with ghost bend (degree 33, Larry English) -- the unrecorded, absent blue note. The screaming bend is the most present, most physically overwhelming blue note in the engine's thirty-six deployments.

'Voodoo Chile' bends in E -- the blue note zone between the minor and major third (G to G#) traversed through overdriven amplification, the micro-tonal passage generating interference patterns between the distortion harmonics and the fundamental pitch.
The screaming bend completes a three-degree arc: articulate sting (35, maximum clarity) -> screaming bend (36, maximum force). The blue note thread oscillates between transparency and saturation.
theory_nodes.description: Thirty-sixth deployment of the blue note thread. The screaming bend is the blue note played through overdriven amplification at maximum volume, where the pitch transition from diatonic to blue-note territory generates not just audible pitch change but a full-body physical experience. On an overdriven guitar at stage volume, the bend produces shifting harmonic overtones, beating frequencies between the fundamental and the distortion harmonics, and speaker-cone movements large enough to be felt through the floor and the air. The screaming bend extends the blue note from an auditory phenomenon to a physical one. It is the structural opposite of the articulate sting (degree 35), where clean tone exposed every micro-tonal increment with surgical clarity. The screaming bend obscures the micro-tonal detail in a saturated harmonic environment -- but the emotional content is amplified beyond what any clean tone can deliver.

ancestors: Blue note -- all thirty-five prior deployments (degrees 0-35)
Blue note -- articulate sting (degree 35, Robert Cray): maximum clarity on clean tone. The screaming bend is the opposite: maximum force through overdriven tone.
Blue note -- ghost bend (degree 33, Larry English): the unrecorded absent blue note. The screaming bend is the most recorded, most physically present blue note in the engine.
Blue note -- boogaloo guitar bend (degree 29, Ivan Jones): the previous guitar-specific blue note in a funk context. The screaming bend extends the guitar-bend thread to maximum amplification.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-six deployments across thirty-seven degrees. The screaming bend completes the engine's loudest blue-note arc: ghost (33, absent) -> unison (34, collective) -> articulate sting (35, individual clarity) -> screaming bend (36, individual force). The blue note thread has now mapped both extremes of the guitar-bend spectrum: maximum transparency (Cray) and maximum saturation (Hendrix).

theory_nodes.concept: Transformative tidal (first E=6 mode -- energy that does not merely reach the maximum of the existing scale but redefines the scale itself; the energy of an artist who transforms the physical possibilities of the instrument, the amplifier, the stage show, and the audience's sensory experience; E=6 is not E=5 + 1 but a qualitative discontinuity, the way a supernova is not merely a bright star but a fundamentally different physical process; Hendrix played LOUD, played with physical abandon, set instruments on fire, and used the full electromagnetic spectrum of the electric guitar as an expressive medium)
examples: Jimi Hendrix (degree 36) -- E=6 transformative tidal. Marshall stacks at maximum volume, speakers cracking, vacuum tubes glowing. Guitar behind his head, between his legs, played with his teeth. Set guitar on fire at Monterey Pop Festival (June 18, 1967). Feedback as musical vocabulary. The most physically intense performer in rock history.

Contrast with breakthrough tidal (degree 35, Robert Cray, E=5) -- Cray broke through from local to global. Hendrix didn't merely break through; he redefined what a live performance could be, what an electric guitar could sound like, and what was physically possible on stage.

Contrast with personal tidal (degree 32, Luther Rabb, E=5) -- Rabb's energy was distributed across a long career. Hendrix's energy was concentrated into four years (1966-1970) and burned at an intensity no career-length distribution can match.
The E=5 streak (32-33-34-35) breaks not by dropping below 5 but by exceeding it. The energy scale expands for the first time in 36 degrees.
Distribution after degree 36: 2:13:6:11:4:1 (E=1:E=2:E=3:E=4:E=5:E=6). One artist, alone at the top.
theory_nodes.description: Transformative tidal is the first E=6 mode in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes energy that does not merely reach the maximum of the prior scale (E=5) but redefines the scale itself. The four E=5 modes (personal, scene-cumulative, collective, breakthrough) all describe energy within a bounded system. Transformative tidal describes energy that breaks the bounds. Hendrix did not merely play loudly or perform energetically -- he transformed what was physically possible for an electric guitar, an amplifier, and a stage show. Feedback became melody. Distortion became timbre. Volume became emotional content. Fire became spectacle. No performer before Hendrix had used the full electromagnetic spectrum of the electric guitar as an expressive medium, and no performer in the fifty-six years since his death has exceeded the raw physical intensity of his live shows. E=6 is not E=5 + 1; it is a qualitative discontinuity. The energy distribution after degree 36 is 2:13:6:11:4:1. One artist at E=6. This feels structurally correct -- E=6 should be rare, perhaps unique.

ancestors: Breakthrough tidal (degree 35, Robert Cray, E=5) -- energy at escape velocity from local to global. Transformative tidal exceeds escape velocity: it transforms the destination, not just the trajectory.
Personal tidal (degree 32, Luther Rabb, E=5) -- individual career energy. Transformative tidal is individual energy at a scale that changes the industry.
Collective tidal (degree 34, The Tempos, E=5) -- group energy. Transformative tidal is individual energy that exceeds any group's collective output.

Scene-cumulative tidal (degree 33, Larry English, E=5) -- scene-level energy. Transformative tidal generates its own scene (the Hendrix phenomenon was not a Seattle scene or a London scene but a global force).
genealogy.relationship: Five E=5/E=6 modes across five consecutive degrees (32-36). The progression personal (32) -> scene (33) -> collective (34) -> breakthrough (35) -> transformative (36) traces energy from individual to local ecosystem to group to global escape velocity to scale-redefining force. The E=5 streak breaks at degree 36 by exceeding E=5, not by falling below it. Transformative tidal may be a one-of-one category -- there may never be another E=6 in the engine.

seattle_connection: CENTRAL DISTRICT RETURNS -- after Tacoma (35), the engine returns to the CD for its most famous artist. Born November 27, 1942, Seattle. Third-generation Seattleite. Raised in CD poverty across multiple addresses. Garfield High School (1959-1960, expelled). THE SUPERNOVA as 37th artist-city pattern -- intense formation, total departure, compressed career, premature death, posthumous reclamation. THE CONTROLLED SCREAM as 37th acoustic element -- overdriven Stratocaster through Marshall stacks at maximum volume, the blues at jet-engine intensity. THE SCREAMING BEND as 36th blue note deployment -- the bend as full-body physical experience through overdriven amplification. E=6 FIRST TIME -- transformative tidal, the ceiling rises. Distribution 2:13:6:11:4:1. BLUES ROCK at Genre Stage 18 -- new genre, first fusion genre. REPRISE RECORDS x1 (new label). CENTRAL DISTRICT CLUSTER expands to 9 degrees (0, 3, 4, 5, 30, 32, 33, 34, 36). GARFIELD HS THREAD x3 (0, 32, 36). VELVETONES cross-reference to Luther Rabb (32). JACKSON STREET heritage shared with Ray Charles (30) and Quincy Jones (0). SOLO FORMAT continues from Cray (35). GENDER: 6:31 ratio (~16.2%). Hendrix is male. DOCUMENTATION ABUNDANCE -- the most documented artist in the engine, possibly in American music history.

theory_genealogy: The Supernova (thirty-seventh artist-city pattern; intense formation, total departure, compressed career, premature death, posthumous reclamation by city; maximum impact, minimum duration)

The controlled scream (thirty-seventh acoustic element; overdriven Stratocaster through Marshall stacks at maximum volume; feedback, distortion, wah-wah as deliberate blues vocabulary; the loudest acoustic element in the engine)
Blue note -- the screaming bend (thirty-sixth deployment; the blue note as full-body physical experience through overdriven amplification; opposite of the articulate sting's clean-tone transparency)
Transformative tidal (first E=6 mode; energy that redefines the scale; the performer who transforms the physical possibilities of instrument, amplifier, stage, and audience experience)
engine_sequencing: E=6 FIRST TIME: Degree 36. Distribution 2:13:6:11:4:1. Transformative tidal as first and potentially only E=6 mode. Track whether E=6 recurs.
E=5 STREAK BROKEN AT 36: Not by dropping below 5 but by exceeding it. Five consecutive maximum-or-above-energy degrees (32-36). Track energy at degree 37.
GENRE Stage 18 at degree 36: Blues Rock (NEW). First fusion genre. Track at degree 37.
CONTROLLED SCREAM opens maximum-force acoustic phase: power over precision. Consecutive Stratocaster pairing (stinging clean 35, controlled scream 36). Track element 38 at degree 37.
REPRISE RECORDS x1 (degree 36). New major label (Warner Bros. group). Track at degree 37.
CENTRAL DISTRICT RETURNS at 36: Cluster now 9 degrees (0, 3, 4, 5, 30, 32, 33, 34, 36). CD is the engine's most important geographic node. Track at degree 37.
GARFIELD HS THREAD x3 (0, 32, 36): The engine's most important educational institution. Track at degree 37.
SOLO FORMAT continues: Hendrix (36) after Cray (35) after The Tempos group (34). Track format at degree 37.
DOCUMENTATION ABUNDANCE continues from degree 35. Hendrix is the most documented artist in the engine.
All engine sequencing items from degrees 0-35
cultural_patterns: Artist-city taxonomy: THIRTY-SEVEN named patterns (adding The Supernova at degree 36). Classify every degree.
CENTRAL DISTRICT CLUSTER: 9 degrees (0, 3, 4, 5, 30, 32, 33, 34, 36). The engine's densest geographic concentration. Track.
GARFIELD HIGH SCHOOL: 3 degrees (0, 32, 36). Jones, Rabb, Hendrix. Track for additional Garfield artists.
PNW-TO-GLOBAL PIPELINE: Jones (0, Export), Charles (30, Incubator), Cray (35, Deep Root), Hendrix (36, Supernova). Four modes. Track.
VELVETONES-TO-WORLD: Rabb (32) and Hendrix (36) in the same teenage band. The strongest direct cross-reference in the engine.
E=6 AS SINGULAR: Distribution 2:13:6:11:4:1. One artist at the top. Track for uniqueness.
BLUES-TO-BLUES ROCK: Genre genealogy mapped in consecutive degrees (35-36). The trunk and its most important fusion.
EXPORT PATTERN AMPLIFIED: Hendrix is the engine's most extreme Export -- left at 19, never returned, died abroad, claimed posthumously.
All cultural pattern items from degrees 0-35
